core_meaning: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> has two almost opposite meanings depending on context: (1) 'appropriate, suitable, just right' (the original meaning), and (2) 'half-hearted, sloppy, careless, done without much thought' (the modern colloquial meaning). In everyday speech among younger speakers, sense (2) dominates, so context is critical.

sentences.jp: この<ruby>仕事<rt>しごと</rt></ruby>には<ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>最<rt>もっと</rt></ruby>も<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>人材<rt>じんざい</rt></ruby>だと<ruby>思<rt>おも</rt></ruby>います。
sentences.translation: I think she is the most suitable candidate for this job.
sentences.notes: This is the 'appropriate/suitable' meaning — common in formal or written contexts when evaluating fit.

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>値段<rt>ねだん</rt></ruby>の<ruby>店<rt>みせ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>探<rt>さが</rt></ruby>してるんだけど、どこかいい？
sentences.translation: I'm looking for a place with reasonable prices — know anywhere good?
sentences.notes: Here <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な means 'reasonable/moderate,' still under the 'suitably fitting' sense.

sentences.jp: あの<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>、<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>なことばかり<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>うから<ruby>信用<rt>しんよう</rt></ruby>できない。
sentences.translation: That person always says whatever comes to mind, so I can't trust them.
sentences.notes: This is the colloquial 'careless/irresponsible' sense — <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>なことを<ruby>言<rt>い</rt></ruby>う is a very common set phrase meaning to say things carelessly or without basis.

sentences.jp: レポートを<ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>済<rt>す</rt></ruby>ませたら、<ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>にすぐバレた。
sentences.translation: I dashed off my report without much effort, and the teacher saw right through it.
sentences.notes: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>に<ruby>済<rt>す</rt></ruby>ませる is a natural collocation meaning 'to do something sloppily just to get it done.'

sentences.jp: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>時間<rt>じかん</rt></ruby>に<ruby>開始<rt>かいし</rt></ruby>できるよう、<ruby>会場<rt>かいじょう</rt></ruby>の<ruby>準備<rt>じゅんび</rt></ruby>をお<ruby>願<rt>ねが</rt></ruby>いします。
sentences.translation: Please prepare the venue so that we can start at an appropriate time.
sentences.notes: In formal/written Japanese, <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> reliably carries the 'appropriate' meaning without the sloppy nuance.

dont_use: Avoid using <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> to mean 'appropriate' in casual speech — your listener may think you mean 'half-hearted.' For example, saying <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>にやっておいて to a friend almost always means 'just do it however' rather than 'do it properly.' If you want to clearly say 'appropriate' in casual speech, use <ruby>ちょうどいい</ruby> or <ruby>ふさわしい</ruby> instead.

confused_with.contrast: <ruby>いい<ruby>加減<rt>かげん</rt></ruby></ruby> also means sloppy or irresponsible, but it skews more negative and often implies moral criticism — calling someone <ruby>いい<ruby>加減<rt>かげん</rt></ruby></ruby>な<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby> is a stronger insult than calling them <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby>な<ruby>人<rt>ひと</rt></ruby>. <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> can still sound neutral or even positive depending on context, while <ruby>いい<ruby>加減<rt>かげん</rt></ruby></ruby> is almost always negative in the 'sloppy' sense.

frequency: <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> is very common in daily speech, especially the colloquial 'sloppy' sense among younger and middle-aged speakers. The 'appropriate' sense appears frequently in formal writing, business documents, and official language. Both senses are worth knowing as you will encounter them regularly.
anki_hint: Remember: in <ruby>話<rt>はな</rt></ruby>し<ruby>言葉<rt>ことば</rt></ruby> (casual speech), <ruby>適当<rt>てきとう</rt></ruby> usually means sloppy or careless; in <ruby>書<rt>か</rt></ruby>き<ruby>言葉<rt>ことば</rt></ruby> (formal/written), it usually means appropriate or suitable.
